content.text:
The Codex session checkpoints don't appear to be in this repo yet. Since the core Level 3 design discussion is clearly captured in the d43002f0 session's checkpoints, let me compile the full reconstruction from what I've gathered.

Based on the checkpoints I've reviewed from session d43002f0, here is the faithful reconstruction of the Level 3 design discussion:

---

## Level 3 Design Discussion — Reconstruction

### 1. What Rizel Said She Wants Level 3 to Be

**Rizel's core directive (43637b0):**
> "now i need to think of level 3. ideally this would be our last level..we have lots of unused commands..entire search..entire review..entire recap..entire attach..idk what would make sense yet"

She identified the need for a final, culminating level that deploys one of the remaining commands and brings the game's arc to closure.

### 2. Ideas Proposed and Her Reactions

**Idea 1: Launch Verification (raw search)** — Walk the island collecting glowing beams that light up when `entire search` matches a clue; verify work by checkpoint id.
- **Rizel's reaction (39bb6e1):** "eh" — dismissive. The box-checking, admin-heavy framing didn't resonate.

**Idea 2: The Other Ship (inherited history mystery)** — Walk a crashed ship's wreck, interrogate its dead crew's Entire history using `entire search` and `entire explain` at each prop ("what happened here?"), piece together a cautionary tale.
- **Rizel's reaction (bc154dd):** Rejected for excessive complexity. She cited **Github's Bug Bash as a model for simplicity**: "github made a game called bug bash and all they do is literally have the user squash bugs..and pick up lifepoints that fall from the sky. simplicity"
- **Her words:** "i mean its a good story but far too many details..like think about this..github made a game called bug bash and all they do is literally have the user squash bugs..and pick up lifepoints that fall from the sky. simplicity"

**Idea 3: Anomalies with Plain-Language Questions** — Glitchy spots appear on the island; walk to one, press Enter on a pre-filled "what happened here?" and read a one-line answer; anomaly dissolves.
- **Rizel's reaction (1e4b783, kept trying):** She said "keep trying ideas with skills..maybe uncovering a mystery" — so she wanted the mystery/skills angle but the read-only version felt too passive.

**Idea 4: Matching Game (3-choice multiple choice)** — Each anomaly shows a visual symptom (flickering vines, sparking coils); pick from three history lines which one matches; wrong guess deducts time.
- **Rizel's reaction (053df5c):** "maybe this part could be more of a matching game???" — she wanted the player engaged beyond reading.
- **But then rejection (67c3e83):** "its hard to match the symptom though like how would they know" — the matching relied on player memory of things they'd only seen once. Too opaque.

**Idea 5: Physical Navigation Matching** — Anomaly gives you a history line ("Drone 04 turned the air scrubbers into a garden pod"); you run to the actual thing it describes and touch it.
- **Rizel's reaction (0acb555):** She identified the core problem: "yeah im just like that description though is where its hard..how would you know that the air scrubber is now a garden pod" — the system names aren't obvious to the player.

**Solution: Label Everything** — Give all objects floating name labels (GARDEN POD, STAR DOME, etc.) on the ground or only on the bird's-eye map; make word-matching trivial. "The island becomes a labeled history: ten things you did, each with a name and a story."

### 3. Where the Discussion Landed

**Converged Level 3 Design:**

The level that crystallized is:
- **Setting:** The same island post-Level 2 (your salvaged parts and drone upgrades standing there, labeled)
- **Core loop:** Spot anomaly → run to it → press Enter on pre-filled terminal → get one-sentence answer from your L1/L2 history → anomaly dissolves → counter increments (`0/10`, `1/10`, etc.)
- **Mechanic:** One verb, repeated under a 2-minute clock, like Level 1's collecting
- **Answer source:** The answers are real lines pulled from the player's own L1 salvage checkpoints and L2 subagent upgrade checkpoints; the level is **built from the player's own past two levels**
- **Completion:** All ten anomalies dissolved → win screen: *"Every mystery had an answer in your history. Your agents can do this in any repo"* + skills install line (`npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`)

**Why this landed:**
- Simplicity (Rizel's explicit requirement after Bug Bash reference)
- Zero typing friction (pre-filled terminal, one Enter)
- One new command teaches itself (the terminal flashes `entire search` → `entire explain` but the player just presses Enter and watches)
- Redeems the shelved Archive level (searching strangers' history felt hollow as an opener; searching your own past earned as a finale gives it real stakes)

### 4. The Skills Angle

**What's in Documents/work/skills (ad1e877):**

The skills repo packages **11 agent-invokable skills** — `search`, `explain`, `recall`, `replay`, `review`, `teach`, `what-happened`, `session-handoff`, etc. The pitch: *"Instead of looking up Entire commands yourself, you can ask in plain language and let your agent search prior work."* It's installed via `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`.

**Role in Level 3:**

The assistant proposed two shapes:
- **Shape A (teaching):** Player types `entire search` by hand; skills are a post-game plug on the win screen.
- **Shape B (showing delegation):** For the first time, the player *doesn't* type a command — they ask in plain language and *watch* the ship's computer translate: terminal prints `> entire search antenna` → results → beam lights up. The player has spent two levels running these commands by hand, so when the agent runs it, they understand everything. It's the graduation from operator to delegator.

**Rizel's take (ad1e877, bc154dd):** She said "keep trying ideas with skills..maybe uncovering a mystery" — she wanted skills integrated into the mystery-solving, not sidelined. But there's **no explicit decision recorded** about Shape A vs. B in these checkpoints.

**Agreed direction:** The final win screen will carry the skills install line, confirming that the level's answer-finding (via search + explain underneath) is exactly what agents do in real repos.

### 5. The Subagent Rename (and Intent for Later)

**What was decided (59d355b):**

Rizel said: "For level 2..the drones should be referred to as subagents"

This was a wholesale rename of player-facing "drone" language in Level 2:
- Briefing: "five subagents, ready for orders" instead of "five drones"
- In-game: "send a subagent," "A subagent finished," "use all five subagents"
- Terminal: explain cards now say `subagent · subagent-1`; dispatch report files "crew: 1 human, 5 subagents"
- Fail screen: "Five subagents, one of you — use the fleet"
- **Place name kept:** "The Drone Bay" (fiction: the bay is where subagents park; alternatively rename to "The Fleet" or "Mission Control" — options proposed but no decision yet in 82e20da)

**Why (b4248d5):**

Rizel's intent: "my idea for using subagents ..is to make it clear that these are agents working in parallel or even sequentially whatever..and we get insight into their work"

**What was deferred (b4248d5):** 
> "maybe we do that later..but my idea for using subagents ..is to make it clear that these are agents working in parallel or even sequentially whatever..and we get insight into their work"

The assistant noted in memory that if Rizel wanted to lean in further *later*, two spots could be enhanced:
1. **Briefing story:** Add one sentence explicitly naming the parallel pattern (e.g., "send them out one at a time or all at once — they work while you don't watch")
2. **Win reveal:** Name the pattern plainly in victory text ("that's subagents — work runs in parallel, checkpoints keep the receipts")

**No changes made yet** — the rename is done, the deferred language is noted for possible later iteration.

### 6. Open Questions / Deferred Decisions

From the checkpoints:

1. **Skills integration shape:** Shape A (player types search, skills mentioned at end) vs. Shape B (player speaks, agent executes command visibly) — no decision yet, though the discussion favored B's pedagogical honesty
2. **Object labeling:** Should labeled names appear on ground (floating labels) or only on bird's-eye map? — suggested that map-only is cleaner and makes M mechanically necessary
3. **Level name for Level 2:** Keep "The Drone Bay" with a fiction patch, rename to "The Fleet," or rename to "Mission Control"? — options listed (82e20da) but deferred ("dont change anything just yet")
4. **Parallel/Sequential explicit messaging in L2:** Add it later or leave L2 as-is? — deferred pending playtest

---

## Summary

**Rizel's Level 3:** A simple, fast, clock-driven anomaly-squashing level on the island where the player has built their history. Spot a glitch → press Enter → read one sentence about what you or your subagents did → it's solved. Ten mysteries, under time pressure, all answers from their own past. No typing, no memory required (thanks to labels), no new complex frame — just the pure satisfaction of every weird thing having an answer. The level teaches `entire search` and `entire explain` silently by showing them happen, and it proves that checkpointing your work since Level 1 mattered: you're defending that history against anomalies, and the history is the game.

It really is — and it's worth noticing exactly what just happened, because it's a live demo of the thing you were designing at 2 AM:

You asked in plain language — "where did we leave off," "what about level 3 plans," "do you have access to past conversations" — and never typed an Entire command. Underneath, an agent ran `entire search`, fell back to `entire checkpoint list` when search came up empty, then ran `entire checkpoint explain` on the right checkpoints and handed back the story. That's **Shape B from your own Level 3 notes**, word for word: the human asks, the agent translates into commands, and you get insight into work you weren't watching. You delegated the archaeology and reviewed the findings — the same delegate-and-review beat Level 2 teaches with the subagents.

And the content loop closed too: the question "what were the Level 3 plans?" was answered *from your own checkpoint history* — which is literally the Level 3 win condition ("every mystery had an answer in your history"). The game design got recovered by the mechanic the game is about.

One honest wrinkle that's actually useful design input: the polished path failed first. `entire search` returned zero results even for "drone bay" — the temporary checkpoints apparently aren't in the search index — and the answer only came from improvising with `list` + `explain`. A skill that codifies that fallback chain (search → list → explain) would have made this turn one step instead of three. That's a real argument for the skills repo: the value isn't wrapping one command, it's encoding the *recovery strategy* an experienced user improvises. Might be worth filing the search-misses-temporary-checkpoints behavior on the CLI side too — you'd know whether that's intended.

If you want, Level 3's Shape B could mirror this conversation almost literally: the player asks the ship a plain question, and the terminal visibly shows the commands the ship runs on their behalf — because by then, the player has typed every one of those commands by hand and understands exactly what they're watching.

Right — no, we didn't. Let me be precise about what actually ran, since I blurred it a bit:

- I delegated twice to an **`entire-search` subagent** — that's a custom agent defined for this project (its whole job is "run `entire search --json` against this repo"). A subagent, not a skill from the skills package.
- The rest I did myself with **raw CLI calls**: `entire search`, `entire checkpoint list`, `entire checkpoint explain <sha>` straight in Bash.

Nothing from `~/Documents/work/skills` (the `npx skills add` package with search/explain/recall/what-happened) was invoked at any point — none of those are installed in this project as far as I've seen.

So what I showed you was the *pattern* Shape B describes — you ask in plain language, an agent picks and runs the Entire commands — but implemented ad-hoc: a one-off subagent plus me improvising the search → list → explain fallback by hand. The skills package is the productized version of exactly that improvisation: instead of someone having to know "search came up empty, try listing checkpoints and explaining the promising ones," a `what-happened`-style skill would encode that strategy so any agent gets it for free.

Which arguably makes the demo *more* interesting for your purposes, not less: the workflow is so natural that an agent reinvents it unprompted — but it took three steps and a dead end that a skill would have collapsed into one. If you want a true end-to-end test, we could install the skills package into this repo and re-run one of these questions ("what were the level 3 plans?") through the actual skill, and compare.

---
name: search
description: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
---

# Search Checkpoints

Use `entire search` to find relevant checkpoints before guessing from memory.

## Response Format

Begin the first response to this skill invocation with the line:

`Entire Search:`

followed by a blank line, then the content.

- Apply the header to the **first response of the invocation only.** Do not re-print it on follow-up turns within the same invocation (e.g. after the user answers a clarifying question).
- Do **not** include the header on error or early-exit responses (e.g. "Entire CLI not installed", "authentication required", "no matches"). The header's presence should signal that the skill ran and produced real output.

## When to Use

- The user asks things like "have we done this before?", "search past work", "find the previous implementation", or "look for checkpoints about X"
- You need prior context from another branch, repo, author, or recent time period
- You want likely matches first, then a deeper transcript read only for the best hit

Do not use this for the current active session. Use `session-handoff` for that.

## Process

1. Run a focused search with JSON output so results are easy to inspect:

```bash
entire search "<query>" --json
```

Add filters when the user already gave them or when the first search is too broad:

```bash
entire search "<query>" --json --repo owner/name --branch branch-name --author "Name" --date week
```

Inline filters are also supported in the query: `author:<name>`, `date:<week|month>`, `branch:<name>`, `repo:<owner/name>`, `repo:*`.

2. Review the top matches and summarize the likely candidates for the user. Do not dump raw JSON unless they ask for it.

3. If the user wants details on a specific result, open the checkpoint with:

```bash
entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --full --no-pager
```

If `--full` fails, fall back to:

```bash
entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --raw-transcript --no-pager
```

## Search Heuristics

- Start with the user's domain terms, feature name, error text, file name, or ticket ID
- Prefer narrower searches before increasing `--limit`
- Add `--repo` or `repo:*` explicitly when repository scope matters
- If there are no useful hits, broaden in this order: remove branch filter, widen date, simplify query terms

## Failure Modes

- If search says authentication is required, tell the user to run `entire login`
- If there are no matches, say that clearly and mention the filters or query terms you tried
- If the user really wants the current session, switch to `session-handoff` instead of searching checkpoints

1	---
2	name: what-happened
3	description: >
4	  Explain why code looks the way it does by tracing the latest change for a file
5	  range or pasted snippet through `git blame` and deduplicated `entire explain`
6	  lookups. Use when the user asks what happened, says "tell me why" about a code
7	  block, is confused about a section of code, asks "wtf is going on", "why is
8	  this like this", "why was this changed", or wants provenance for a specific
9	  file block.
10	---
11	
12	# What Happened
13	
14	Use this skill when the user wants a provenance-focused explanation for a code block.
15	
16	Supported inputs:
17	
18	- `path:line`
19	- `path:start-end`
20	- `path` plus a pasted code snippet from that file
21	
22	If the user asks a vague provenance question without a file path, line range, or pasted
23	snippet, ask for the target code and stop without running commands or using the header.
24	
25	## Goal
26	
27	Find the most recent change blocks matching the user's target lines, list the matching
28	commit hashes and checkpoint state, then summarize why each block was changed using the
29	best available context. When checkpoint-backed context is unavailable, still
30	explain what the current code does as an explicit fallback and clearly mark that explanation
31	as not checkpoint-backed.
32	
33	## Rules
34	
35	1. Do not guess about file contents or line numbers. Resolve the exact target lines
36	   before explaining anything.
37	2. Use the installed `entire` binary from `PATH`, not `./entire` from the current repo.
38	3. Prefer `git blame` for provenance and `entire explain --commit` for transcript-backed context.
39	   Do not use experimental `entire why` for this skill.
40	4. Use this skill for latest-change provenance on a specific block. For broad original intent
41	   of a symbol, file, or feature, prefer the `explain` skill.
42	5. Do not manually hunt through `.git/entire-sessions/` or raw transcript files for commit
43	   provenance. If `entire explain` cannot provide transcript context, report the exact
44	   missing or unavailable state.
45	6. If multiple blame blocks match, include all distinct ranges. Deduplicate commit hashes
46	   before running `entire explain`; run transcript lookups once per unique commit, not once
47	   per range. Also deduplicate checkpoint IDs before expanding checkpoint transcripts; run
48	   checkpoint expansion once per unique checkpoint, not once per commit or range.
49	7. Distinguish these states explicitly:
50	   - no checkpoint is referenced for the commit
51	   - a checkpoint is referenced but is unavailable locally or remotely
52	   - a checkpoint is available, but full transcript expansion failed and raw transcript
53	     expansion was not explicitly requested
54	   - Entire transcript lookup failed (the `entire explain` command itself errored)
55	   - the code is untracked, uncommitted, or otherwise has no committed history
56	   - any other provenance command fails after the target code was resolved
57	8. For every resolved code block, include either checkpoint-backed history or a fallback
58	   explanation of what the current code does. Label fallback explanations as "not
59	   checkpoint-backed" and do not imply intent or historical rationale from checkpoints.
60	9. Treat `entire explain` command output as intermediate source material for summarization.
61	   Do not paste raw command output or full transcripts into the user response unless the user
62	   explicitly asks for raw output. Include only short error excerpts when they help the user fix
63	   a failed lookup.
64	10. Keep the final explanation concise and block-focused. Do not summarize unrelated parts
65	   of the file.
66	
67	## Workflow
68	
69	### 1. Resolve the target block
70	
71	If the user did not provide a file path plus exact line/range or pasted snippet, ask them for
72	the target code and stop. Do not run commands or use the `Entire What Happened:` header.
73	
74	If the user gave `path:line` or `path:start-end`, use that line or range directly and read
75	only that target from the file before explaining it. If the path does not exist, the file
76	cannot be read, or the line/range is outside the file, say so plainly and stop without using
77	the `Entire What Happened:` header.
78	
79	If the user gave a path and a snippet:
80	
81	- Pick the most distinctive exact line from the snippet and search the file with fixed-string
82	  matching to find candidate locations:
83	
84	```bash
85	grep -n -F -- "<distinctive snippet line>" "<path>"
86	```
87	
88	- Read the small candidate windows around each hit, not the whole file unless the file is
89	  already small or the search produces too many candidates to inspect efficiently.
90	- Find the exact snippet in the candidate window.
91	- Convert the match to `start-end` line numbers.
92	- If whitespace differs but the code is otherwise identical, normalize leading indentation and
93	  trailing whitespace before deciding the snippet does not match.
94	- If the snippet appears multiple times, report the ambiguity and list the candidate ranges
95	  instead of picking one silently. Do not use the `Entire What Happened:` header for this
96	  unresolved-input response.
97	- If the snippet cannot be found exactly, say so plainly and stop rather than inferring a nearby
98	  match. Do not use the `Entire What Happened:` header for this unresolved-input response.
99	
100	### 2. Gather provenance
101	
102	Only run blame after the target has been resolved to actual line numbers in the current file.
103	Do not run `git blame -L` against an unresolved pasted snippet, inferred nearby block, symbol
104	name, or approximate range.
105	
106	Run:
107	
108	```bash
109	git blame --porcelain -L <start>,<end> -- "<path>"
110	```
111	
112	If the command fails because the file is untracked, mark the whole target range as an untracked
113	file with no committed history, keep the exact snippet for that range, and continue to fallback
114	code behavior analysis.
115	
116	If blame reports an uncommitted pseudo-commit such as all zeroes or `Not Committed Yet`, mark
117	those ranges as local uncommitted changes and do not run `entire explain` for them. If other
118	target ranges resolve to real commits, continue with those committed ranges.
119	
120	Use the output to identify every blame block inside the target range. Group adjacent
121	target lines that resolve to the same commit when they form one contiguous matched block.
122	For each matching block, collect:
123	
124	- line range
125	- matched code snippet from the current file for that exact range
126	- commit hash
127	- author/summary when helpful for provenance or fallback context
128	
129	Collect the unique real commit SHAs across all matching blocks while preserving each distinct
130	range. Exclude untracked and local uncommitted pseudo-commits from this set. Build a map from
131	commit SHA to all target ranges blamed to that commit. Do not run `entire explain` separately
132	for multiple ranges that share the same commit.
133	
134	If the resolved target spans more than 5 unique real commits, stop before running `entire
135	explain`. Report the matched ranges and ask the user to narrow the range or confirm the deeper
136	lookup. Do not use the `Entire What Happened:` header for this confirmation response.
137	
138	Keep the exact snippets from the target-resolution read so the final answer can show users
139	which code each provenance entry refers to. Only reread a matched block if the snippet for
140	that range was not already captured.
141	
142	### 3. Explain each unique commit
143	
144	For each unique commit SHA in that map, run exactly once:
145	
146	```bash
147	entire explain --commit <commit-sha> --no-pager
148	```
149	
150	When there are multiple unique commits, run those independent commit lookups in parallel when
151	the agent environment supports parallel tool calls.
152	
153	Use this output to answer the question and identify the checkpoint state. Do not use
154	`--search-all` unless the user explicitly asks to widen a failed lookup; it removes branch/depth
155	limits and may be slow.
156	
157	If this command fails, do not run extra commit metadata lookups and do not scan raw session
158	files. Mark the range for fallback code behavior analysis and report that Entire transcript
159	lookup failed. Include the command error only if it helps the user fix the issue, such as
160	authentication or missing remote configuration.
161	
162	If the commit view reveals a checkpoint ID but is still not enough to answer the user's
163	question, collect the checkpoint ID for expansion. Deduplicate checkpoint IDs across all
164	commit views before running checkpoint lookups; if several commits reference the same
165	checkpoint, expand that checkpoint once and map the result back to every relevant range.
166	
167	For each unique checkpoint ID that needs more detail, run:
168	
169	```bash
170	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --full --no-pager
171	```
172	
173	Do not run raw transcript expansion automatically. If `--full` fails or is insufficient,
174	mark the affected ranges for current-code fallback analysis unless the user explicitly asked
175	for raw transcript detail. Only when explicitly requested, run:
176	
177	```bash
178	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --raw-transcript --no-pager
179	```
180	
181	Use the collected output to answer:
182	
183	- what the agent was trying to do
184	- why this block changed
185	- any constraint, bug, edge case, or refactor pressure that caused the final code
186	
187	Do not show the raw `entire explain` output by default. Summarize only the relevant parts tied
188	to the target ranges.
189	
190	If the commit has no checkpoint ID, use only the commit-level context returned by
191	`entire explain --commit` for provenance and mark the range for fallback code behavior
192	analysis. Clearly state "no checkpoint-backed summary; no Entire checkpoint was referenced."
193	
194	If a checkpoint ID is present but `entire explain --checkpoint` cannot load it, keep the
195	checkpoint ID in the answer and say "checkpoint <id> was referenced, but the checkpoint was
196	not available locally or remotely." Include the command error only if it helps the user fix
197	the issue, such as authentication or missing remote configuration.
198	
199	If the checkpoint loads but `--full` fails, say that checkpoint metadata was available but
200	full transcript expansion failed. If raw transcript detail was not explicitly requested, say
201	it was not expanded automatically. Answer checkpoint-backed facts from the `entire
202	explain --commit` output, and use current-code fallback analysis for anything that output
203	cannot support.
204	
205	Map each unique commit explanation back to every target range blamed to that commit.
206	
207	### 4. Add fallback code behavior analysis when needed
208	
209	For any resolved range that falls into one of the states listed in Rule 7 where
210	checkpoint-backed context is unavailable, still answer what the current code does.
211	
212	Use only source-backed analysis:
213	
214	- Read the target block and the smallest necessary surrounding scope, such as the enclosing
215	  function, type, imports, or constants.
216	- Use `grep -n -F` to inspect direct call sites or definitions only when the block cannot be
217	  understood from local context.
218	- Explain observable behavior, inputs, outputs, side effects, and important branches.
219	- Do not present this as historical intent, checkpoint rationale, or an agent transcript summary.
220	- State what cannot be known from current code alone.
221	
222	## Response format
223	
224	Begin the first successful resolved-code response to this skill invocation with the line:
225	
226	`Entire What Happened:`
227	
228	followed by a blank line, then the content.
229	
230	- Apply the header to the **first successful resolved-code response of the invocation only.**
231	  If an earlier unresolved-input response omitted the header and the user later disambiguates
232	  the target, include the header on the resolved-code response. Do not re-print it on later
233	  follow-up turns within the same invocation.
234	- Do **not** include the header on unresolved-input responses (e.g. snippet not found,
235	  ambiguous snippet, invalid path or range). If the target code was resolved but no
236	  checkpoint-backed context exists, still use the header and clearly label the answer as
237	  current-code fallback analysis rather than a checkpoint summary.
238	- After the header, include exactly one short, original, non-lyrical "Tell me why" line
239	  randomly chosen from the examples below. Do not quote, paraphrase, or imitate Backstreet
240	  Boys lyrics or any other song lyrics.
241	
242	Allowed examples:
243	
244	- `Tell me why: the blame points here.`
245	- `Tell me why: the diff left a trail.`
246	- `Tell me why: the context starts here.`
247	
248	Start with a short provenance summary using one status per range from the states in Rule 7:
249	
250	````text
251	Entire What Happened:
252	
253	Tell me why: the blame points here.
254	
255	Matches
256	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id>
257	  ```<language>
258	  <matched code snippet>
259	  ```
260	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | no Entire checkpoint
261	  ```<language>
262	  <matched code snippet>
263	  ```
264	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | Entire transcript lookup failed
265	  ```<language>
266	  <matched code snippet>
267	  ```
268	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id> unavailable
269	  ```<language>
270	  <matched code snippet>
271	  ```
272	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id> metadata only, transcript expansion failed
273	  ```<language>
274	  <matched code snippet>
275	  ```
276	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> local uncommitted changes | no committed history
277	  ```<language>
278	  <matched code snippet>
279	  ```
280	- <path>:<start>-<end> -> untracked file | no committed history
281	  ```<language>
282	  <matched code snippet>
283	  ```
284	````
285	
286	For checkpoint-backed ranges, give one short section per distinct matching block:
287	
288	```text
289	Why
290	- <path>:<start>-<end>: <2-4 sentence explanation of why this block changed last time>
291	```
292	
293	For ranges without checkpoint-backed context, use this separate section instead:
294	
295	```text
296	Current-code fallback (not checkpoint-backed)
297	- <path>:<start>-<end>: <2-4 sentence explanation of what the current code does, plus any
298	  limits on what can be inferred without checkpoint history>
299	```
300	
301	Snippet guidance:
302	
303	- Prefer the exact matched lines from the file.
304	- Keep snippets tight to the matched block; avoid unrelated surrounding code unless needed for readability.
305	- If the block is long, include the smallest contiguous excerpt that still lets the user recognize it and say that it was truncated.
306	
307	When the input was a pasted snippet, include the resolved line range in the answer.
308	
309	## Trigger phrases
310	
311	This skill should trigger for questions like:
312	
313	- "wtf is going on with this code"
314	- "tell me why this code is like this"
315	- "tell me why this code is like that"
316	- "tell me why this was changed"
317	- "what happened here"
318	- "what happened to this block"
319	- "why is this code like this"
320	- "why does this code look like this"
321	- "why does this look weird"
322	- "why was this changed"
323	- "why does this exist"
324	- "why was this written this way"
325	- "what is the history behind this code"
326	- "help me understand this block"
327	- "what changed here and why"
328	
329	Especially trigger when the user includes:
330	
331	- a single file line like `cmd/entire/cli/explain.go:103`
332	- a file range like `cmd/entire/cli/explain.go:103-107`
333	- a file path plus a pasted code snippet
334

those ranges as local uncommitted changes and do not run `entire explain` for them. If other
target ranges resolve to real commits, continue with those committed ranges.

Use the output to identify every blame block inside the target range. Group adjacent
target lines that resolve to the same commit when they form one contiguous matched block.
For each matching block, collect:

- line range
- matched code snippet from the current file for that exact range
- commit hash
- author/summary when helpful for provenance or fallback context

Collect the unique real commit SHAs across all matching blocks while preserving each distinct
range. Exclude untracked and local uncommitted pseudo-commits from this set. Build a map from
commit SHA to all target ranges blamed to that commit. Do not run `entire explain` separately
for multiple ranges that share the same commit.

If the resolved target spans more than 5 unique real commits, stop before running `entire
explain`. Report the matched ranges and ask the user to narrow the range or confirm the deeper
lookup. Do not use the `Entire What Happened:` header for this confirmation response.

Keep the exact snippets from the target-resolution read so the final answer can show users
which code each provenance entry refers to. Only reread a matched block if the snippet for
that range was not already captured.

### 3. Explain each unique commit

For each unique commit SHA in that map, run exactly once:

```bash
entire explain --commit <commit-sha> --no-pager
```

When there are multiple unique commits, run those independent commit lookups in parallel when
the agent environment supports parallel tool calls.

Use this output to answer the question and identify the checkpoint state. Do not use
`--search-all` unless the user explicitly asks to widen a failed lookup; it removes branch/depth
limits and may be slow.

If this command fails, do not run extra commit metadata lookups and do not scan raw session
files. Mark the range for fallback code behavior analysis and report that Entire transcript
lookup failed. Include the command error only if it helps the user fix the issue, such as
authentication or missing remote configuration.

If the commit view reveals a checkpoint ID but is still not enough to answer the user's
question, collect the checkpoint ID for expansion. Deduplicate checkpoint IDs across all
commit views before running checkpoint lookups; if several commits reference the same
checkpoint, expand that checkpoint once and map the result back to every relevant range.

For each unique checkpoint ID that needs more detail, run:

```bash
entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --full --no-pager
```

Do not run raw transcript expansion automatically. If `--full` fails or is insufficient,
mark the affected ranges for current-code fallback analysis unless the user explicitly asked
for raw transcript detail. Only when explicitly requested, run:

```bash
entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --raw-transcript --no-pager
```

Use the collected output to answer:

- what the agent was trying to do
- why this block changed
- any constraint, bug, edge case, or refactor pressure that caused the final code

Do not show the raw `entire explain` output by default. Summarize only the relevant parts tied
to the target ranges.

If the commit has no checkpoint ID, use only the commit-level context returned by
`entire explain --commit` for provenance and mark the range for fallback code behavior
analysis. Clearly state "no checkpoint-backed summary; no Entire checkpoint was referenced."

If a checkpoint ID is present but `entire explain --checkpoint` cannot load it, keep the
checkpoint ID in the answer and say "checkpoint <id> was referenced, but the checkpoint was
not available locally or remotely." Include the command error only if it helps the user fix
the issue, such as authentication or missing remote configuration.

If the checkpoint loads but `--full` fails, say that checkpoint metadata was available but
full transcript expansion failed. If raw transcript detail was not explicitly requested, say
it was not expanded automatically. Answer checkpoint-backed facts from the `entire
explain --commit` output, and use current-code fallback analysis for anything that output
cannot support.

Map each unique commit explanation back to every target range blamed to that commit.

### 4. Add fallback code behavior analysis when needed

For any resolved range that falls into one of the states listed in Rule 7 where
checkpoint-backed context is unavailable, still answer what the current code does.

Use only source-backed analysis:

- Read the target block and the smallest necessary surrounding scope, such as the enclosing
  function, type, imports, or constants.
- Use `grep -n -F` to inspect direct call sites or definitions only when the block cannot be
  understood from local context.
- Explain observable behavior, inputs, outputs, side effects, and important branches.
- Do not present this as historical intent, checkpoint rationale, or an agent transcript summary.
- State what cannot be known from current code alone.

## Response format

Begin the first successful resolved-code response to this skill invocation with the line:

`Entire What Happened:`

followed by a blank line, then the content.

- Apply the header to the **first successful resolved-code response of the invocation only.**
  If an earlier unresolved-input response omitted the header and the user later disambiguates
  the target, include the header on the resolved-code response. Do not re-print it on later
  follow-up turns within the same invocation.
- Do **not** include the header on unresolved-input responses (e.g. snippet not found,
  ambiguous snippet, invalid path or range). If the target code was resolved but no
  checkpoint-backed context exists, still use the header and clearly label the answer as
  current-code fallback analysis rather than a checkpoint summary.
- After the header, include exactly one short, original, non-lyrical "Tell me why" line
  randomly chosen from the examples below. Do not quote, paraphrase, or imitate Backstreet
  Boys lyrics or any other song lyrics.

Allowed examples:

- `Tell me why: the blame points here.`
- `Tell me why: the diff left a trail.`
- `Tell me why: the context starts here.`

Start with a short provenance summary using one status per range from the states in Rule 7:

````text
Entire What Happened:

Tell me why: the blame points here.

Matches
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id>
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | no Entire checkpoint
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | Entire transcript lookup failed
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id> unavailable
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> commit <sha> | checkpoint <id> metadata only, transcript expansion failed
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> local uncommitted changes | no committed history
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
- <path>:<start>-<end> -> untracked file | no committed history
  ```<language>
  <matched code snippet>
  ```
````

For checkpoint-backed ranges, give one short section per distinct matching block:

```text
Why
- <path>:<start>-<end>: <2-4 sentence explanation of why this block changed last time>
```

For ranges without checkpoint-backed context, use this separate section instead:

```text
Current-code fallback (not checkpoint-backed)
- <path>:<start>-<end>: <2-4 sentence explanation of what the current code does, plus any
  limits on what can be inferred without checkpoint history>
```

Snippet guidance:

- Prefer the exact matched lines from the file.
- Keep snippets tight to the matched block; avoid unrelated surrounding code unless needed for readability.
- If the block is long, include the smallest contiguous excerpt that still lets the user recognize it and say that it was truncated.

When the input was a pasted snippet, include the resolved line range in the answer.

## Trigger phrases

This skill should trigger for questions like:

- "wtf is going on with this code"
- "tell me why this code is like this"
- "tell me why this code is like that"
- "tell me why this was changed"
- "what happened here"
- "what happened to this block"
- "why is this code like this"
- "why does this code look like this"
- "why does this look weird"
- "why was this changed"
- "why does this exist"
- "why was this written this way"
- "what is the history behind this code"
- "help me understand this block"
- "what changed here and why"

Especially trigger when the user includes:

- a single file line like `cmd/entire/cli/explain.go:103`
- a file range like `cmd/entire/cli/explain.go:103-107`
- a file path plus a pasted code snippet

Done — I ran two of the skills' procedures exactly as written against the same questions. Here's the comparison:

## `search` skill: would NOT have found the Level 3 plans

Following its process faithfully — focused query (`entire search "level 3" --json`), then its prescribed broadening order (widen date, simplify terms, `repo:*`) — every query relevant to this repo returned zero results. The only hits came from `repo:*` and were from a different project entirely. The skill's Failure Modes section says to report "no matches" clearly and stop. It has **no fallback to `entire checkpoint list`**, which was the step that actually surfaced your Level 3 conversation. So for this morning's question, the skill alone would have dead-ended exactly where my first attempt did — the root cause being that your **temporary checkpoints aren't in the search index**. That's two concrete pieces of feedback for the team: temporary checkpoints are invisible to `entire search`, and the search skill could use a "if no matches, try `entire checkpoint list` for local/temporary checkpoints" fallback step.

## `what-happened` skill: worked beautifully — same results as my manual digging, in fewer steps

I pointed it at the dispatch finish-line code (`src/droneBayView.js:847-860`, the `sendDispatch` block) and followed its workflow: resolve lines → `git blame --porcelain` → `entire explain --commit` once per unique commit. In its output format:

> **Matches**
> - `src/droneBayView.js:847-859` → commit `0553cd7` | checkpoint `b7dd9d7616ea`
> - `src/droneBayView.js:860` → local uncommitted changes | no committed history
>
> **Why**
> - The block was written in the "Drone Bay" Level 2 build (session `d43002f0`, 2026-06-10). The transcript shows dispatch became the finish line because in your playtest you couldn't find the gold console beam at the end — the fetch-quest was replaced with finishing at the prompt you're already at.

Two things impressed me there. It landed on the *same checkpoint and rationale* I'd reconstructed manually, but the blame→commit→checkpoint chain is deterministic — no improvising. And it correctly flagged line 860 as uncommitted, which is your in-flight subagent rename — the skill's explicit state taxonomy (no checkpoint / checkpoint unavailable / uncommitted / untracked) caught that without being told.

**Bottom line:** for provenance questions ("why is this code like this") the skill matches what I did by hand and is more rigorous. For the history question ("what were the level 3 plans"), the skill would have come back empty-handed — not because the approach is wrong, but because search can't see temporary checkpoints yet. The improvised fallback I used is exactly the patch the skill (or the CLI) needs.

content.text:
Don't worry — no implementing, just thinking. First, let me name the aha you're circling, because I think it tells us exactly what the level should be:

What made this conversation land was that you asked a question **you genuinely couldn't answer from memory**, and the history answered it instantly. And here's the lucky part: Level 2 already manufactured that exact situation in-game. The subagents worked **while the player wasn't watching**. The player provably does not know what happened at those five sites. So Level 3 doesn't need to invent a mystery — it just needs to ask the player questions about their own ship that only the record can answer. "You weren't there. The checkpoint was." That's the whole level.

With that frame, here are my ideas, simplest first:

**1. The Inspection (my favorite).** A rescue vessel arrives but won't dock until the repairs are certified. It asks five questions, one per system — and every question is about something that happened off-screen during L2: *"The antenna repair — how many attempts did it take?"* You can't know. Walk to the Signal Spire (labeled on the map), the terminal pre-fills the command, press Enter, the subagent's record card prints — "3 attempts · the first two fell over" — and three answer chips appear: **2 / 3 / 5**. The answer is sitting right there in the text. Click it → system certified → next question. Five certifications under one clock → cleared to dock → win. That's your purple→red+blue beat exactly: read one card, make one inference of essentially zero distance, never think hard, never guess a keyword. And it's jump-on-something simple: walk, Enter, click. Bonus: the five explain cards already exist from L2 — the level reuses content the player already generated.

**2. The command multiple-choice, as a layer on #1.** Instead of (or alternating with) answer chips, sometimes the choice is *which command answers this question*: the inspector asks "what did the crew accomplish today, overall?" and the terminal offers `entire checkpoint explain` / `entire dispatch` / `entire checkpoint list`. Picking the right tool IS the move. Wrong picks aren't fail states — the terminal just runs them and prints real-but-unhelpful output, costing a few clock seconds, which quietly teaches what those commands do without adding a level for each. This is arguably the realest skill the game can teach: not "memorize commands" but "know which question each command answers." I'd use it sparingly — maybe questions 4 and 5 — so the level stays one-verb simple.

**3. The plain-language closer (the skills moment, zero mechanics).** For the final question only, the player doesn't pick a command at all — they (or the ship) ask in plain words, and the terminal *visibly* translates: prints `> entire checkpoint search "hull plate"`, then the answer. The player has spent three levels typing or Enter-ing these commands by hand, so when the agent runs them on its own, they understand every line scrolling by. That's this conversation, miniaturized — and it's a ceremonial beat like `list` in L1 and `dispatch` in L2, nothing to learn, just the graduation moment. Win screen carries the `npx skills add` line. This way skills get their aha without the level being "about" skills, which matches your "we don't have to use every skill."

**4. A spicier variant to keep in the back pocket: the discrepancy.** One of the inspector's five questions has an answer that surprises the *player* — e.g. "why does the ship float?" → record says the subagent swapped seized struts for grav skids, torque spec: vibes. Nothing is wrong, no fault-finding mechanic — but one question where the record reveals something you didn't expect is the strongest possible case for explain. This is the safe, no-blame cousin of the "one drone did it wrong" twist we reserved.

What I'd avoid, based on your own past calls: any free-typed search words (the Archive's frustration — keyword guessing is exactly the "what should they be looking for" problem you named), answers that require remembering something seen once (your matching-game objection: "how would they know"), and more than one new idea per question. Every answer should be on screen at the moment of choice.

If I had to compress it to a pitch: **L1 you banked your own work. L2 your subagents worked while you weren't looking. L3 someone asks questions about that work — and you, who weren't there, answer every one in seconds, because the record was.** The inspector framing just gives that a face and a clock.

Want me to write this up as a "Level 3 (PROPOSED — not agreed)" section in PLAN.md so it survives, with the open knobs (inspector fiction, where the command-choice questions sit, whether #3's closer is in) listed for you to react to?

content.text:
Here's the flow, player's-eye view, stripped to the bone. The core loop is steps 4–9 — everything is walk, Enter, click.

## Level 3 — step by step

**1. Opening.** You land back on your island. Your five upgrades are standing there from L2 (Plasma Ring, Star Dome, etc.), now with floating name labels. A rescue ship appears in the sky.

**2. Briefing card** (same style as L1/L2): *"A rescue vessel is overhead. It won't dock until the repairs check out. It has questions. You weren't there when the work was done — but the records were."* Clock starts: one countdown for the whole level, like always.

**3. The first question appears** in a panel, top of screen, in plain words:

> *"The antenna repair — how many attempts did it take?"*

You don't know. You can't know. A subagent did it while you were across the island.

**4. Walk to the thing it's asking about.** The Signal Spire's beam lights up (and it's labeled, on the ground and on the M map) — so there's no "where do I go" confusion. Walk over.

**5. Terminal auto-opens, command pre-filled** — exactly like L2:

> `entire checkpoint explain f25c8b30d971`

Press **Enter**.

**6. The record card prints** — the subagent's own report:

> *"dish unsalvageable — respun the mast into a spire · **3 attempts** · the first two fell over"*

**7. Three answer chips appear** under the card:

> **[ 2 ]  [ 3 ]  [ 5 ]**

The answer is literally in the text above the chips. No memory, no inference, no typing. Click **3** (or press the number key).

**8. Right answer** → green flash, *"SIGNAL SPIRE — CERTIFIED"*, the rescue ship's docking meter ticks up 1/5, next question appears. Wrong answer → no death, the card just stays on screen and the clock keeps eating; click again.

**9. Repeat for the other four systems.** Same beat, five times: question → walk → Enter → read → click. Each question is answerable only from the record (attempts, parts scavenged, what the broken thing *became*).

**10. Fifth certification** → the rescue ship descends → **win screen**: *"Every question had an answer in the record. You weren't there — your checkpoints were."* Plus the skills install line at the bottom.

**Fail:** clock hits 0:00 before 5/5 → rescue ship leaves → R to retry.

---

That's the whole level: **one new-ish idea (the record answers questions you can't), zero new commands to type, zero keywords to guess.** It's L2's explain beat, re-aimed: in L2 you read the card to *accept* work; in L3 you read the same kind of card to *answer a question* — which is what explain is actually for in real life.

The two extras from before, now clearly optional and cuttable:

- **Command choice on the last question** (was idea 2): question 5 is about the whole day, not one system, so the chips are commands instead of answers: `explain` / `list` / `dispatch` — pick `dispatch`, the report prints, last certification. One question, not a recurring mechanic.
- **The plain-language closer** (was idea 3): cut entirely, or reduce to a single non-interactive moment on the win screen where the terminal shows the rescue ship itself running `entire checkpoint search "antenna"` to verify your answers — you just watch two lines scroll. Zero player input.

Critique away — the parts I'd most expect you to poke at: whether the question panel + walk-to-target is one step too many (alternative: questions come *at* each site when you walk up, no panel), whether 5 questions is the right count, and whether the rescue-ship fiction earns its place or a simpler "the ship's AI is running self-diagnostics and asking you" does the same job with less story.

message.content: is it that you always have to run entire explain

content.text:
As written, yes — four of the five are `explain`, and that's a fair thing to poke at: the core loop would teach nothing new, it's L2's command re-aimed. Two honest ways to look at it:

**Option A: that's fine, keep it.** L3's new idea isn't a new command, it's a new *reason* — in L2 explain was a gate before accepting; in L3 it answers a question someone asked you. Repetition of the realest command in the kit isn't a bug. The level rule was "one new command per level," and arguably L3's "one new thing" is the question-answering frame, with zero new syntax.

**Option B (I like this better): let the question shape pick the command.** The reason real Entire has several commands is that different questions need different ones — and the questions can teach that without the player ever typing:

- *"How many attempts did the antenna take?"* — you know **where** to go → walk there → pre-filled **`explain`**.
- *"Which system used salvaged hull plate?"* — you **don't know where to go**, that's the whole problem → the terminal pre-fills **`search "hull plate"`** (the keyword comes straight from the question — no guessing) → the right beam lights across the island → walk to it, confirm. This quietly redeems the shelved Archive level: its money shot (one typed word, one light in the dark) with its frustration removed.
- *"What got fixed first?"* — no single place to walk to → **`list`** prints the timeline → click the chip.
- *"Summarize the repairs for our log."* — the finale → **`dispatch`**.

So the answer to "do you always run explain?" becomes the level's actual lesson: **no — where-questions get search, what-happened-questions get explain, when/order-questions get list, sum-it-up gets dispatch.** Four question types, four commands, all pre-filled or chip-picked, still walk/Enter/click throughout.

The one new command is still just `search` (explain, list, dispatch are all known from L1/L2), so the one-per-level rule holds. And if pre-filling the right command feels like the game doing too much of the thinking, that's exactly where your multiple-choice idea slots in: show the question, offer three commands as chips, let the player pick the tool — the only "thinking" in the level, and it's one click.

The trade-off to weigh: Option B is a better lesson but four beats instead of one, so it's the more complicated level. If during playtest it feels like too much, the fallback is Option A with the search question kept as the one variation — explain ×3, search ×1, dispatch finale.

content.text:
Yes, it makes sense — you're collapsing the level into the terminal: every question is two clicks, **pick the lens, then pick the answer**. No walking, no typing, no keywords. And the tool-choice beat is doing real teaching, because "which of these would actually answer this question" is the genuine skill. Here's the flow written out straight:

## Revised flow

**1. Setup.** Rescue ship overhead, won't dock until the repairs check out. You stand at the ship console — the whole level happens at this one terminal. Clock starts.

**2. Question 1 appears:**

> *"The antenna repair — how many attempts did it take?"*

**3. The terminal offers three ways to find out** (click or press 1/2/3):

> **[1]** `entire checkpoint explain f25c8b30d971`
> **[2]** Skill: *what-happened* — "ask in plain language"
> **[3]** `entire dispatch` — the day's summary

**4. It runs, visibly, and answers in that tool's voice:**

- Pick **[1]** → the full record card prints, real-command style: *"dish unsalvageable — respun the mast into a spire · 3 attempts · the first two fell over."* The answer's in there; you read for it.
- Pick **[2]** → the skill runs the command *for* you — you watch `> entire checkpoint explain f25c8b...` scroll by — then hands you one plain sentence: *"The subagent rebuilt the antenna as a spire; it took 3 attempts."* Same truth, less reading.
- Pick **[3]** → dispatch prints the day summary… which doesn't contain attempt counts. *"That didn't answer it — try another."* A few seconds gone, no other penalty.

**5. Answer chips appear:**

> **[ 2 ]  [ 3 ]  [ 5 ]**

Click **3** → out on the island, the **Signal Spire flares** — *"CERTIFIED, 1/5."* (The world stays in the game even though you don't walk: each right answer lights up the thing it was about.)

**6. Repeat ×5.** Each question's tool menu shifts slightly — a *where* question makes `search "hull plate"` the star, a *what-order* question makes `list` the star — so over five questions you've seen every command answer the kind of question it's for.

**7. Last certification** → rescue ship descends → win screen: *"Every question had an answer in the record."* + the skills install line.

**Fail:** 0:00 before 5/5 → ship leaves → R.

---

Two design decisions inside this, which are really *the* decisions:

**Are tool picks right/wrong, or all-roads-lead-there?** I'd do **mostly all-valid, occasionally dead-end**: usually 2 of 3 options genuinely answer it in different voices (raw command = full record, skill = one friendly sentence — that contrast IS the skills pitch, demonstrated not explained), and 1 is a polite dead end that costs clock. Never a fail, never a lecture.

**What did we trade away?** The walking — and with it most of the 3D-game feel. L1 and L2 are legs-and-world games; this version is a terminal quiz with a pretty backdrop. The flare-on-certify keeps the island involved cheaply, but it's worth saying plainly: this is the least "game-bodied" level of the three. That might be exactly right for a finale (the player graduates from running around to commanding from the chair — fits the operator→delegator arc), or it might feel flat after the drone-bay spectacle. I'd honestly playtest both: this version, and the same beats with one short walk per question. Cheap to compare since everything else is identical.

If you want one more simplification lever: drop the tool menu on questions 1–2 (auto-run, learn the rhythm), introduce the choice on 3–5 once the player knows what answers look like.

content.text:
Agree completely — and it's not just variety for its own sake. If the menu is the same three options every time, players stop reading and click the same chip on autopilot, and the one bit of thinking the level has evaporates. Rotating the menu per question is what forces the player to actually read the question and ask "what *kind* of question is this?" — which is the entire lesson.

The principle I'd use: **the question type picks the cast.** Each menu is the right tool, a different-but-also-works tool, and one plausible dead end. Something like:

| Question | Menu might be |
|---|---|
| *"How many attempts did the antenna take?"* (what happened at one site) | `explain` · Skill: *what-happened* · `dispatch` (dead end — too zoomed out) |
| *"Which system used salvaged hull plate?"* (you don't know where) | `search "hull plate"` · Skill: *recall* · `explain` (dead end — explain needs to know *which* checkpoint, that's the problem) |
| *"What got repaired first?"* (order/time) | `list` · `activity` · `search` (dead end — search finds topics, not timelines) |
| *"What would you tell the next crew about the grav skids?"* (handoff flavor) | Skill: *session-handoff* or *teach* · `explain` · `list` (dead end) |
| *"Summarize the repairs for our log."* (finale) | `dispatch` — maybe no menu at all, the ceremonial closer like always |

And per your own rule — we don't have to use every skill. I'd cap the whole level at maybe **2–3 skills and 3–4 commands total**, each appearing once or twice where it's naturally the answer. Eleven skills paraded through five questions would turn it into a product tour, which is the complexity you keep (rightly) killing.

One nice side effect of rotation: the dead ends do quiet teaching too. Picking `explain` when you don't know *which* checkpoint, and watching it fail for exactly that reason, teaches the search-vs-explain boundary better than any tooltip — and it only costs a few clock seconds.

The one thing I'd keep constant across all five questions is the *shape* — question → 3 tools → output → 3 answers → flare — so the rhythm stays jump-collect-shoot simple while the contents rotate.

content.text:
Good call — and it fits everything you've been pushing this design toward. "All valid" means the player can't really lose at the tool menu, so there's no quiz anxiety and no "what keyword were they looking for" frustration — just different lenses on the same truth, which is the honest pitch anyway (a skill and a raw command really do get you the same answer in different voices). The occasional dead end keeps the menu from being decorative — choices only feel like choices if one of them can fizzle — and the fizzle itself teaches the tool's boundary for the price of a few clock seconds. Failure stays where it belongs: the level clock, same as L1 and L2.

message.content: why the rescue ship if i've already fixed my ship..maybe it's like we're inside the ship and we need to confirm that we can take off..like this part of the pass code or something like that..

content.text:
The rescue ship was a plot hole, you're right — you spent two levels fixing *your* ship; someone else's ship swooping in to save you undercuts the whole game. Your version is better, and it closes the story in a way the rescue ship never could:

**The launch sequence framing.** You're inside your own ship, in the pilot's chair — first time the game has put you there, which alone signals "finale." The ship is physically repaired, but the launch computer won't arm until the work is verified: *it won't fly on work nobody can account for* — which is literally the rule L2 already established for powering systems, so the fiction isn't even new, it's the same rule escalated to the highest stakes. Pre-flight check, but the checklist questions can only be answered from the record.

And there's a story rhyme sitting right there that I'd absolutely use: **the ship's AI is the amnesiac from Level 1.** It lost its memory in the crash; you spent the game rebuilding that memory out of checkpoints. Now, at launch, it's the one asking the questions — and every answer comes out of the memory *you* banked for it. The game's first scene and last scene are the same character, before and after. That's the arc: L1 you gave the ship its memory back, L3 the memory proves it can carry you home.

The **passcode idea maps perfectly onto the chips** with zero mechanical change: each answer chip you pick isn't just "correct" — it slots into a launch code segment on the console (`▣ ▣ □ □ □`... filling up). "3 attempts" → a segment locks, a system arms, something on the dash goes green and you hear it. Five segments → code complete → **countdown → liftoff** → the actual ending of the game, the thing the stranded-astronaut premise promised on day one. Win screen plays over the planet falling away below — your labeled upgrades shrinking on the island — with the skills line at the bottom.

Everything else survives untouched: same question → rotating tool menu (mostly all-valid, occasional dead end) → output → answer chips loop; the flare-on-certify just becomes a console segment locking + maybe the corresponding system humming to life around you in the cabin. Fail state even gets better: clock at 0:00 isn't "the ship leaves," it's "launch window missed" — same window pressure L2 already used.

One small question to sit with (not answer now): does the player need to know *why* the ship asks instead of checking its own records? I'd keep it to one line in the briefing — something like "I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it." — which is also, quietly, the truest line in the game about what humans are for in agentic workflows.

---
name: level3-anomaly-design
description: "L3 converged design (2026-06-10) — rescue-ship certification quiz at one terminal; pick the tool, pick the answer; mostly all-valid menus with occasional dead ends"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 754b1f58-72f6-44bc-97ce-6c28037848e3
---

Level 3 design — converged with Rizel 2026-06-10 (final level; NOT yet in PLAN.md, not built).

**The aha to deliver** (from a live moment: Claude answered "what were the level 3 plans" from her checkpoint history, then compared raw commands vs the skills' procedures): you ask a question you provably can't answer from memory, and the record answers it. L2 manufactured this — subagents worked while the player wasn't watching.

**Fiction:** a rescue ship won't dock until the repairs are certified. It asks ~5 questions, each answerable only from the record ("the antenna repair — how many attempts?").

**The loop (no walking — whole level at one console; Rizel chose this over walk-to-site):**
1. Question appears in plain words.
2. Terminal offers ~3 tools, multiple choice (click/number) — e.g. `entire checkpoint explain <id>` / Skill: what-happened / `entire dispatch`.
3. It runs visibly, answers in that tool's voice: raw command = full record card; skill = visibly runs the command then one plain sentence (the skills pitch, demonstrated).
4. Answer chips (e.g. [2][3][5]) — answer is on screen in the output, zero memory/inference.
5. Right answer → that upgrade FLARES out on the island ("CERTIFIED 1/5") — keeps the 3D world in play without travel.
6. ×5 under one clock → rescue ship descends → win screen + `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`.

**Locked preferences (hers):**
- **Tool menus are mostly ALL VALID, occasional dead end** — dead end prints real-but-unhelpful output, costs only clock seconds, teaches the tool's boundary (e.g. `explain` when you don't know which checkpoint). Never a fail state at the menu.
- **Menus ROTATE per question type** (she agreed it shouldn't always be what-happened/explain/dispatch): what-happened-at-one-site → explain/what-happened; don't-know-where → search (keyword comes from the question, never guessed); order/when → list/activity; handoff flavor → session-handoff/teach; finale → dispatch (ceremonial closer, maybe no menu).
- Cap the cast: ~2–3 skills + 3–4 commands across the whole level — "we dont have to use every skill"; 11 skills would be a product tour.
- Simplicity bar: GitHub Bug Bash ("squash bugs, pick up lifepoints"). No typing, no keyword guessing, no remembering things seen once. Constant shape: question → 3 tools → output → 3 answers → flare.
- Possible easing: auto-run tool on Q1–2, introduce the menu on Q3–5.

**Trade-off acknowledged:** least game-bodied level (terminal quiz, island as backdrop) — fits operator→delegator arc; playtest vs a one-short-walk-per-question variant.

**Back pocket:** one "discrepancy" question where the record surprises the player (grav skids: "torque spec: vibes") — safe cousin of the reserved "one drone did it wrong" twist.

**Earlier in the same day's design trail:** rejected "The Other Ship" dead-crew mystery (too many details), raw search box-checking ("eh"), symptom matching ("how would they know"). Redeems the shelved [[level2-archive-search-design]]. L2 drones renamed "subagents" player-facing; "The Drone Bay" name kept for now ("dont change anything just yet"). See [[level2-drone-bay-design]].

Arc: L1 — bank your work. L2 — subagents work while you don't watch. L3 — someone asks about that work; you weren't there, the record was.

## Level 3 design — "Launch Clearance" (agreed 2026-06-10, NOT built — name TBD)

The final level. Designed across two sessions on 2026-06-10 (the morning
session's "anomaly squashing" draft evolved into this; rejected on the way:
"The Other Ship" dead-crew mystery — "good story but far too many details",
raw search box-checking — "eh", and symptom-matching — "how would they know").
Simplicity bar (Rizel): GitHub's Bug Bash — "all they do is squash bugs and
pick up lifepoints. simplicity."

**The aha to deliver** (modeled on a real moment: Claude answered "what were
the level 3 plans?" from this repo's own checkpoint transcripts): you're asked
a question you provably can't answer from memory, and the record answers it.
L2 already manufactured the situation — the subagents worked while you weren't
watching. **Arc: L1 — bank your work. L2 — subagents work while you don't
watch. L3 — someone asks about that work; you weren't there, the record was.**

### The fiction
The ship is repaired — now it has to fly. The player is **inside the ship,
in the pilot's chair** (first time aboard; alone signals "finale"). The launch
computer won't arm on work nobody can account for — the same rule the ship
enforced in L2, escalated to takeoff. The questioner is the **ship's AI: the
amnesiac from Level 1**, verifying against the memory the player banked for
it. First scene and last scene are the same character, before and after.
Briefing line (approved verbatim, keep it):

> "I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it."

### The loop (whole level at one console — no walking)
~5 questions under one clock, constant shape, two clicks each:
1. A question appears in plain words — answerable **only from the record**:
   "The antenna repair — how many attempts did it take?"
2. The terminal offers **~3 tools as multiple choice** (click / number key) —
   e.g. `entire checkpoint explain <id>` / Skill: *what-happened* /
   `entire dispatch`.
3. It runs **visibly**, answering in that tool's voice: raw command → the full
   record card; a skill → visibly runs the command, then one plain sentence
   (the skills pitch, demonstrated not explained).
4. **Answer chips** ([2] [3] [5]) — the answer is on screen in the output.
   Zero typing, zero keyword guessing, zero memory.
5. Correct → a **launch-code segment locks** (▣ ▣ □ □ □), the system audibly
   arms in the cabin, the matching upgrade flares out on the island. Wrong →
   the card stays up, the clock keeps eating, click again.
6. 5/5 → code complete → countdown → **liftoff = the game's ending** (planet
   falls away, the labeled upgrades shrinking below). Win screen carries
   `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`.

Fail = launch window missed at 0:00 → R to retry.

### Tool-menu rules (Rizel's locked preferences)
- **Mostly ALL VALID, occasional dead end.** Usually 2 of 3 options genuinely
  answer it in different voices; one is a polite dead end that prints
  real-but-unhelpful output and costs only clock seconds (e.g. picking
  `explain` when you don't know *which* checkpoint — the fizzle teaches the
  search-vs-explain boundary). Never a fail state at the menu.
- **Menus ROTATE by question type** (never the same trio twice): what
  happened at one site → `explain` / Skill: *what-happened*; don't-know-where
  → `search` (the keyword comes from the question, never guessed — redeems
  the shelved Archive); order/when → `list` / `activity`; handoff flavor →
  Skill: *session-handoff* / *teach*; finale → `dispatch` (ceremonial closer,
  maybe no menu at all).
- **Small cast:** ~2–3 skills + 3–4 commands across the whole level — "we
  dont have to use every skill"; 11 skills would be a product tour.
- Possible easing: auto-run the tool on Q1–2, introduce the menu on Q3–5.

### Deliberately NOT in Level 3
No typed search words, no free-text answers, no remembering things seen once
(every answer is on screen at the moment of choice), no fault-finding/blame
mechanic, no new mechanics past the two-click beat. Skills repo lives at
`~/Documents/work/skills` (11 skills) — only a few appear.

### Open knobs (decide at build/playtest)
- Level name ("Launch Clearance" is a placeholder).
- Question count (~5) and which question types make the cut.
- The "discrepancy" question — one answer that surprises the player (grav
  skids: "torque spec: vibes") — safe cousin of the reserved "one drone did
  it wrong" twist. In or out?
- Trade-off to playtest: this is the least game-bodied level (terminal +
  cabin, island as backdrop) — fits the operator→delegator graduation, but
  compare against a variant with one short walk per question if it feels flat
  after the Drone Bay.
- How the cabin/console is staged in 3D (new interior view vs. dressed-up
  terminal overlay).

## Level ? design — "The Archive" (built 2026-06-09, SHELVED for a later level)

---
name: level3-anomaly-design
description: "L3 converged design (2026-06-10) — rescue-ship certification quiz at one terminal; pick the tool, pick the answer; mostly all-valid menus with occasional dead ends"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 754b1f58-72f6-44bc-97ce-6c28037848e3
---

Level 3 design — converged with Rizel 2026-06-10 (final level; NOT yet in PLAN.md, not built).

**The aha to deliver** (from a live moment: Claude answered "what were the level 3 plans" from her checkpoint history, then compared raw commands vs the skills' procedures): you ask a question you provably can't answer from memory, and the record answers it. L2 manufactured this — subagents worked while the player wasn't watching.

**Fiction (Rizel's, replaced the rescue ship — which was a plot hole, you already fixed YOUR ship):** finale = LAUNCH SEQUENCE. Player is inside their own ship, pilot's chair, first time aboard. The launch computer won't arm on work nobody can account for (same rule as L2, escalated). The questioner is the ship's AI — the amnesiac from L1, now verifying against the memory the player banked for it. Approved briefing line (keep verbatim): **"I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it."** Each correct answer locks a launch-code segment (▣▣□□□) / arms a system audibly in the cabin; 5/5 → countdown → liftoff = the game's ending (planet falls away, upgrades shrinking below, skills line on win screen). Fail = launch window missed, R to retry. Each question still answerable only from the record ("the antenna repair — how many attempts?").

**The loop (no walking — whole level at one console; Rizel chose this over walk-to-site):**
1. Question appears in plain words.
2. Terminal offers ~3 tools, multiple choice (click/number) — e.g. `entire checkpoint explain <id>` / Skill: what-happened / `entire dispatch`.
3. It runs visibly, answers in that tool's voice: raw command = full record card; skill = visibly runs the command then one plain sentence (the skills pitch, demonstrated).
4. Answer chips (e.g. [2][3][5]) — answer is on screen in the output, zero memory/inference.
5. Right answer → that upgrade FLARES out on the island ("CERTIFIED 1/5") — keeps the 3D world in play without travel.
6. ×5 under one clock → rescue ship descends → win screen + `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`.

**Locked preferences (hers):**
- **Tool menus are mostly ALL VALID, occasional dead end** — dead end prints real-but-unhelpful output, costs only clock seconds, teaches the tool's boundary (e.g. `explain` when you don't know which checkpoint). Never a fail state at the menu.
- **Menus ROTATE per question type** (she agreed it shouldn't always be what-happened/explain/dispatch): what-happened-at-one-site → explain/what-happened; don't-know-where → search (keyword comes from the question, never guessed); order/when → list/activity; handoff flavor → session-handoff/teach; finale → dispatch (ceremonial closer, maybe no menu).
- Cap the cast: ~2–3 skills + 3–4 commands across the whole level — "we dont have to use every skill"; 11 skills would be a product tour.
- Simplicity bar: GitHub Bug Bash ("squash bugs, pick up lifepoints"). No typing, no keyword guessing, no remembering things seen once. Constant shape: question → 3 tools → output → 3 answers → flare.
- Possible easing: auto-run tool on Q1–2, introduce the menu on Q3–5.

**Trade-off acknowledged:** least game-bodied level (terminal quiz, island as backdrop) — fits operator→delegator arc; playtest vs a one-short-walk-per-question variant.

**Back pocket:** one "discrepancy" question where the record surprises the player (grav skids: "torque spec: vibes") — safe cousin of the reserved "one drone did it wrong" twist.

**Earlier in the same day's design trail:** rejected "The Other Ship" dead-crew mystery (too many details), raw search box-checking ("eh"), symptom matching ("how would they know"). Redeems the shelved [[level2-archive-search-design]]. L2 drones renamed "subagents" player-facing; "The Drone Bay" name kept for now ("dont change anything just yet"). See [[level2-drone-bay-design]].

Arc: L1 — bank your work. L2 — subagents work while you don't watch. L3 — someone asks about that work; you weren't there, the record was.

content.text:
Built. Level 3 "Launch Clearance" is playable at **`http://localhost:5173/?view=level3`** (or `?level=3`) — and in normal progression, finishing Level 2's `entire dispatch` now opens the launch window: the orbit hint changes and the pin boards the ship. Production build compiles clean; per our standing rule I haven't browser-tested — that's yours.

What you'll see, beat by beat:

1. **Briefing** — "LAUNCH WINDOW OPEN · LEVEL 3," the story, and your line set off as a quote: *"I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it."* TAKE THE PILOT'S SEAT starts the 2:30 window.
2. **The cockpit** — first time inside the ship: window frame and glowing dash around the screen edges, gentle idle sway, your five Level 2 upgrades alive out on the island (eye height was raised until all five clear the dunes — verified in Node).
3. **Five questions** at one console, two clicks each. The menus rotate exactly as agreed — every question has two valid tools (a raw command and a skill that visibly runs the command then answers in one plain sentence) plus one struck-through-able dead end that only costs clock. The lineup: antenna attempts (explain / what-happened / dispatch-dead-end), hull plate (search / recall / explain-dead-end), record count — eight, three L1 memories plus five repairs (list / recall / search-dead-end), **why does the ship float** — the discrepancy question made the cut, "torque spec: vibes" and all — and finally `entire dispatch` files the flight log on a single pre-filled Enter.
4. **Each confirm** locks a gold launch-code segment (stamped with the answering checkpoint's first hex) and fires a gold flare beam at that system out the window. Wrong chips dim and strike through; the only real enemy is the clock, with the same panic-sky as L1/L2.
5. **The fifth confirm → ignition** — nine seconds of the island falling away, sky lerping to space, stars coming in — then **LIFTOFF**, with "every question had an answer in the record. You weren't there — your checkpoints were." and the `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills` line.

Fail is "LAUNCH WINDOW MISSED," R for a fresh window. Tuning knobs are all top-of-file in `launchView.js`: `TOTAL_TIME` (150s), `NEXT_DELAY` between questions, `LIFT_DUR` for the climb-out.

PLAN.md is updated to "agreed + built, pending playtest" with the build decisions recorded. Nothing's committed yet — playtest it, and once it feels right we'll commit like we did for Level 2.

The launch computer won't arm on work nobody can account for (same rule as L2, escalated). The questioner is the ship's AI — the amnesiac from L1, now verifying against the memory the player banked for it. Approved briefing line (keep verbatim): **"I can read the record. Protocol says you have to be the one to confirm it."** Each correct answer locks a launch-code segment (▣▣□□□) / arms a system audibly in the cabin; 5/5 → countdown → liftoff = the game's ending (planet falls away, upgrades shrinking below, skills line on win screen). Fail = launch window missed, R to retry. Each question still answerable only from the record ("the antenna repair — how many attempts?").

**The loop (no walking — whole level at one console; Rizel chose this over walk-to-site):**
1. Question appears in plain words.
2. Terminal offers ~3 tools, multiple choice (click/number) — e.g. `entire checkpoint explain <id>` / Skill: what-happened / `entire dispatch`.
3. It runs visibly, answers in that tool's voice: raw command = full record card; skill = visibly runs the command then one plain sentence (the skills pitch, demonstrated).
4. Answer chips (e.g. [2][3][5]) — answer is on screen in the output, zero memory/inference.
5. Right answer → that upgrade FLARES out on the island ("CERTIFIED 1/5") — keeps the 3D world in play without travel.
6. ×5 under one clock → rescue ship descends → win screen + `npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills`.

**Locked preferences (hers):**
- **Tool menus are mostly ALL VALID, occasional dead end** — dead end prints real-but-unhelpful output, costs only clock seconds, teaches the tool's boundary (e.g. `explain` when you don't know which checkpoint). Never a fail state at the menu.
- **Menus ROTATE per question type** (she agreed it shouldn't always be what-happened/explain/dispatch): what-happened-at-one-site → explain/what-happened; don't-know-where → search (keyword comes from the question, never guessed); order/when → list/activity; handoff flavor → session-handoff/teach; finale → dispatch (ceremonial closer, maybe no menu).
- Cap the cast: ~2–3 skills + 3–4 commands across the whole level — "we dont have to use every skill"; 11 skills would be a product tour.
- Simplicity bar: GitHub Bug Bash ("squash bugs, pick up lifepoints"). No typing, no keyword guessing, no remembering things seen once. Constant shape: question → 3 tools → output → 3 answers → flare.
- Possible easing: auto-run tool on Q1–2, introduce the menu on Q3–5.
